Rewrite it to be concise and scannable, and explain the changes you made.
pages.explanation: A strong answer both tightens the language and adds structure. It cuts filler ('in order to', 'basically', 'went ahead and', 'it should be noted that'), and it breaks the changes into a scannable list, likely with a short summary line and an issue reference.

keyPoints: Removes filler phrases like 'in order to', 'basically', 'went ahead and', 'it should be noted that'
Breaks the list of changes into a bulleted or numbered list instead of one paragraph
Keeps a short, clear summary of the purpose (fixing the login crash)
Preserves essential information including the issue reference (closes #42)
commonMisconceptions: Thinking concise means dropping needed details like the issue link
Believing formatting alone fixes it without also tightening the wording
Assuming a wall of text is fine as long as the words are correct
rubric.sampleAnswer:
**Summary:** Fix login page crash by refactoring the auth module.

**Changes:**
- Refactored the auth module
- Updated tests
- Updated docs

Closes #42.

I cut filler words ('in order to', 'basically', 'it should be noted that'), split the run-on sentence into a scannable bulleted list, and kept the summary and issue reference so reviewers still have the context they need.

lessons.title: Consistency and Callouts
lessons.description: Apply consistent conventions and use notes, warnings, and callouts effectively.

pages.title: Why Consistency Matters
pages.body:
You've learned dozens of Markdown features. Now the challenge shifts from *knowing* syntax to *applying it the same way every time*. **Consistent style** is what makes a document feel professional rather than patched together.

Consistency means picking one convention and sticking to it throughout a document (and ideally across a whole project). A reader shouldn't be distracted by the *how* — they should only notice the content.

Consider a README where headings jump around: one section uses `## Setup`, another uses `## installation`, another writes `## USAGE`. Nothing is broken, but it reads like three people wrote it in a hurry. Compare that to `## Setup`, `## Installation`, `## Usage` — parallel, title-cased, predictable.

Inconsistency creates tiny friction on every line. Multiply that across a long document and readers lose trust in the details.

pages.title: Conventions to Lock Down
pages.body:
Here are the choices worth making once and applying everywhere:

### Emphasis markers
Pick one style for bold and italic. If you write `**bold**` and `_italic_`, don't switch to `__bold__` and `*italic*` halfway through.

### List markers
Choose a single bullet character. Use `-` throughout, or `*` throughout — not a mix:

- Consistent uses `-` every time
- Which keeps the raw source clean

### Heading case
Decide on Title Case (`## Getting Started`) or sentence case (`## Getting started`) and hold the line for every heading.

### Code references
Always wrap file names, commands, and identifiers in `inline code`. If `config.json` is code in one paragraph, it should be code everywhere.

### Spacing
One blank line between blocks, a newline at the end of the file. These invisible habits keep diffs small and clean.

The specific choice matters less than making a choice and honoring it.

pages.prompt: Which snippet shows the best internal consistency?
pages.context: A contributor is drafting a section of a README.

options.text: Uses `-` for every bullet, Title Case for every heading, and `**bold**` throughout.
Mixes `-` and `*` bullets, alternates `**bold**` and `__bold__`, and varies heading case.
options.misconception: You may think variety looks richer, but mixing markers signals carelessness and adds noise to the source.

options.text: Uses Title Case headings but switches bullet characters between every list.
options.misconception: Getting headings right is good, but switching bullet characters still breaks consistency in the raw source.

options.text: Wraps file names in code in one section but leaves them as plain text elsewhere.
options.misconception: Applying a convention only sometimes is exactly the inconsistency to avoid — code formatting must be uniform.

pages.title: Notes, Warnings, and Callouts
pages.body:
Sometimes a line of text needs to *stand out* — a caveat, a heads-up, a critical warning. These are **admonitions** (also called callouts): visually distinct blocks that pull the reader's eye.

### The portable way: blockquotes
Plain Markdown has no built-in callout, so a common convention is a blockquote with a bold label:

> **Note:** This feature requires Node 18 or newer.

> **Warning:** Deleting this file cannot be undone.

This renders anywhere Markdown is supported.

### GitHub-flavored alerts
GitHub, and several other platforms, support a special syntax built on blockquotes:

```
> [!NOTE]
> Useful information the reader should know.

> [!WARNING]
> Critical content demanding immediate attention.
```

The keyword in brackets triggers a colored, icon-labeled box when rendered. Supported types include `NOTE`, `TIP`, `IMPORTANT`, `WARNING`, and `CAUTION`.

pages.title: Choosing a Callout Type
pages.intro: Match the severity of your message to the right callout keyword.
pages.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[What kind of message?] --> B[Helpful extra info]
  A --> C[A better way to do it]
  A --> D[Must not be missed]
  A --> E[Risk of a bad outcome]
  B --> F[NOTE]
  C --> G[TIP]
  D --> H[IMPORTANT]
  E --> I[WARNING / CAUTION]
pages.caption: Notice that callout choice is about the reader's needed reaction — informative vs. cautionary — not just decoration.

pages.prompt: Match each callout type to the situation where it fits best.
pages.explanation: NOTE adds context, TIP offers a shortcut, IMPORTANT flags essential info, and WARNING signals possible harm. Matching severity to keyword keeps callouts meaningful.

pairs.right: Extra background the reader might find useful
An optional shortcut that makes a task easier
Essential info the reader must not skip
An action that could cause data loss or breakage

pages.prompt: Write the GitHub-flavored alert opening line (the blockquote line with the bracketed keyword) that would introduce a critical, attention-demanding callout.
pages.explanation: GitHub alerts start with a blockquote marker, then the keyword in brackets: `> [!WARNING]`. The alert body goes on the following blockquoted lines.
pages.hint: Start with the blockquote character, then the keyword in square brackets prefixed with an exclamation point.

pages.prompt: You're reviewing a teammate's README that uses three different bullet characters, mixes `**bold**` and `__bold__`, and buries a data-loss warning inside a normal paragraph. Explain what you'd advise them to change and why.
pages.explanation: A strong answer connects consistency to reader trust and low-friction reading, and connects callouts to surfacing critical information.

keyPoints: Pick one bullet character and one bold style and apply them uniformly throughout
Consistency reduces reader friction and makes the document look professional/trustworthy
The data-loss warning should be pulled out into a callout/admonition (blockquote with a bold label or a GitHub `> [!WARNING]` alert)
A warning must stand out visually because critical info buried in a paragraph is easily missed
commonMisconceptions: Believing the specific marker choice matters more than making a single consistent choice
Thinking callouts are purely decorative rather than tools to match reader reaction to message severity
rubric.sampleAnswer: I'd tell them to standardize on one bullet character (say `-`) and one bold style (`**bold**`) across the whole file, because the current mix adds noise and makes the doc look careless — consistency keeps readers focused on content. Then I'd move the data-loss warning out of the paragraph into its own callout, like `> [!WARNING]` on GitHub or a `> **Warning:**` blockquote, so a reader can't miss something that could destroy their data.
